sentences.text: So as I told you, he went to the commanding officer, [INAUDIBLE] [?
Leupold, ?] and told him that he's very furious about it and he's very angry, but he wants to settle it by himself.
First of all, because it's his loss.
Excuse me.
It's his loss.
And secondly, he wants to make it the way that others should see how I am punished in order to be afraid of repeating such problems.
And I think he convinced him also-- also by bribing, by drinking, or something, giving him some present.
At the end, he made a trial, and he was the main judge.
His engineer was the second judge.
I don't remember what he said.
It was a full performance.
He was shouting a few minutes and shouting at me, how-- how did you know that such idiots, such-- such [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] men can work in-- in his factory.
But at the end, he said, all-- all right.
It's the first time, so I'm punishing you to do the work four weeks, or something like this, only nights, night shifts.
And then he went down the-- he was sitting a little bit higher than we were-- he went down and kicked me.
But before, he made a  very special sign with his eye to show me that I won't feel too much.
And it really was.
And all the time, he shouted in order to-- that everybody knows and hears that he is shouting.
And that was the end.
So that means that he really saved my life.

There was a period of time when the demographics in the situation was that way, they-- they couldn't send us away on this famous march to death, because the Germans were emptying every camp when the front line was approaching, or was near, near.
They were emptying ever every camp and sending the people to the-- on the march to death.
And not too many survived the march of death.
Now we couldn't be sent, because the front line was such a way that there was no way out of it.
We-- we could only stay or-- or be killed on the place.
So as we were told, there came an order from Berlin to shot-- to finish with us on the place.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is another famous story.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The people thought what to do, again, the people who were influentials and led together with Schindler.
So they came to the conclusion that only one solution is to send away the commanding officer out of the camp.
And to send him away, to bring him to go to somewhere that is a very big probability that he won't come back because of the-- of the-- front line.

So this-- the decision fell, and there was made a false march order for the commanding officer, that he should come to a place where we understood-- or they understood-- that if he came there, he cannot go-- he cannot come back.
And the forge was made-- it was written on the machine-- the special stamp was made in order to make it very real.
And the stamp was made by-- by a man who was with us.
And who afterwards, or now, was a judge in the superior court of Israel.
And it was said somewhere-- he's now retired-- it was said somewhere that he's the only judge in the world who began his career as a forger.
But this succeeded, really, this Mr.
Leupold ?] was sent away.
And at that time, the-- the guards were all the older people.
And the influence of Schindler was 100% on them.
That means he made what he wanted.
Don't forget that this was already winter of '45.
So, again, we were saved.
And now there's small-- small details-- small [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] details.
We're unsure sure how it will work.
So we decided, if we are-- we are in the position to defend ourselves, maybe we will.
And Schindler was bringing weapons to the camp for us.
And they got their weapons in the Czech underground for us, and they brought them.
And they were divided.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had-- I didn't get any, but a very good friend of mine living in the States was one that had one.
And not too many, but there were weapons who were brought by Schindler.

And before the-- the last days of the war, the same Czech-- I didn't say you before that Schindler was very influential by the Germans because he was a very, very, very important spy for Germans in Czechoslovakia and in Poland before the war.
He was traveling between a part of Poland, Czechoslovakia and Germany.
And it seems that he was a very important spy.
And again, as an award, he got the possibility to-- to come to Krakow to get this factory, and so on and so on.
Now when the war was at the end, a few days before the end of the war, the same Czech underground from the surroundings, they knew what he did for us.
They knew how to-- what he was doing during all the time.
So they sent a message and told, we know these are the last days of the war.
And if Schindler is left on the Czech soil after the war is finished, he will be sentenced, because he was-- he was a spy against Czechoslovakia before the war.
But because we know what he did for-- for you, we give him the chance to disappear now, and to go away from Czechoslovakia.
And this was the day-- it was, if I am not mistaken, two days before the end of the war, when he left us-- with 10 of us-- 10 people who worked-- who were-- who were real car mechanics.
So we went-- you-- we-- they went with him, 8, 8 of them all-- I don't remember exactly-- went with him.
It was two cars, with him and his wife.
And they went towards West in order to run away from, first of all, from the Czechs, secondly from the Russians.
And they arrived until the-- they came to the French/Swiss/German border.
And he was-- in the meantime, the war ended, so they changed clothes.
And-- ah, we were in-- in Gross Rosen, we got the-- the stripe uniforms.
So he got one, and his wife got one.
And they were going, because it was on the way, where the Russians were, the Americans were in the first few days.
If they saw a German, they could jail him.
So he went as a-- as a prisoner.
And they came to the border, and this was the end of the story.
But this was the end.
There is a real story, not an anecdote, that my mother-- we suffered-- the last three months, or four months, we-- we suffered very much.
We had not enough to eat.
As I told you, because of the demographic situation, even the-- the food that had to come to us, or that we were supposed to get, couldn't reach us.
And then now the-- and now the courageous steps of Schindler-- we were hungry.
And so we took a car and went close to the front lines, where some villages were abandoned already.
And he robbed meal or whatever he found and brought to us.
And that was-- that was the food we got.
He stole.
He was robbing.
It was-- everything was empty.
It was-- it was open.
So he stole it and brought.
This was the way how we-- how we kept, because we couldn't get any foods.
So one of the victims was-- we were all very [INAUDIBLE].
You know how it was.
But my mother was very ill.
It's very well known that people who suffer from hunger can get very swollen.
And she was terribly swollen.
She was at the end of her-- of her efforts to survive.
And there were a few-- three, four women in such-- in the same stage of-- of being very swollen.
It's called hunger edema, just being swollen from hunger.
And you choke at the end of this.
So Mrs.
Schindler was finding a special, very delicate food.
Some called of oatmeals, or something like this.
One of what he stole somewhere.

And was cooking it in her house, in her place, and was bring, bring-- ah, and my mother was-- with the three or four other women-- was in a special room which was called 'Sick Room.' And she was by herself bringing it and feeding these three or four women and saving them.
What happened when the war ended?
Nobody was left with us, and we were left alone.
And it took about two weeks until the Russians came.
They left.
They left after Schindler left.
They ran away.
They ran away.
And we were left alone.
And then the Russians came and kept us in the camp.
And it took about two or three weeks, and then we were transported, all of us, with the-- again with the cattle train to Poland by the Russians.
Together, it took about three, four days until we were brought to Krakow-- organized.
And most of all ran away immediately.
After that, came to Germany, and so on.
I went to Germany.
I stayed in Germany.
I started in Germany, and then I went to Israel.
I didn't want to stay with the-- with the Russians, first of all.
I didn't want to stay in Poland.
Poland was, for me, the terrible place.
I stayed there for a few days, maybe a week or two.
And I felt very, very not only unhappy.
And I felt that I won't find my place in Poland after what happened.
So there was a big trend to-- to leave to the West.

And there were possibilities to smuggle through the frontiers, through, again, Czechoslovakia to Germany, because most of the people who were fleeing from the camps were gathered in Germany in the so-called DPs, Displaced Persons camps.
So I went there, but I wasn't in the camp.
I began to-- my studies.
And-- Where did you study, and what did you study?
I studied dentistry in the first university in the south of Germany, which opened up after the war-- which was reopened after the war.
They were very rare.
I was the-- the only one still-- I was the first Jewish student on the German university since '33.
And there were-- afterwards, there were a few.
But don't worry, the most of them were Germans.
And after I finished my studies, in the meantime, I met a girl, so we left for Israel in '49.
I did marry my wife in Germany.
Yes.
In August '49.
And Since then, we are in Israel.
We lived the first few years in the region of Tiberius, so-called Jordan Valley.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- our daughter was born there, at that-- in that region.
And after three years, we-- we came to Tel Aviv.
And since then, we live in Tel Aviv.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we came back to Krakow, and we were accepted by a Jewish family who was hiding in-- around Krakow, but they had already a place to live.
They took us to them.
It were friends of my parents.
And then I was walking around the streets of Krakow, and we had-- we had nothing to wear, or very little to wear.
So I went to a place where Poles were selling and buying.
You know, it's a kind of market, where selling and buying goods, but not food, mostly clothes and small furnitures, and this.
It was a very special place in Krakow from before the war, even.
And this was, by the way, my first-- my first meeting with so-called good and bad Poles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met there a woman whom we gave a lot of our clothes before we went to ghetto, because first of all, we couldn't take anything-- too much with us, as I told you before.
And secondly, we thought it won't take too much time.
She was a friend of the family.
She was a very good acquaintance with us.
So when it's over, we will get it back.
And this woman was sitting there and was selling our clothes.
And when she saw me, she immediately recognized me.
I recognized her too.
And not that she didn't apologize, or-- her first sentence, she said-- her first reaction was, you're really-- you're really alive?
Or something like this.
This was the first shock.
Then on the street, you could hear from here and there, too many Jews were left.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I haven't got enough time to know how bad or how good the relations between Jews and Poles could be or will be after the war, because my decision was a very quick one.
And as I said to you, this one accident was enough for me.
The second one was when I met the drunk Russian soldiers, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which I didn't like.
I didn't like the atmosphere, so my decision was very, very final and very quick.
And it didn't take too much time.
I went with my-- ah, in the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a cousin of mine, who-- I told you before that we were left with three cousins.
So one was killed.
The second one came to Krakow.
We were [? all ?] in Krakow.
He came two, three days after us from another cave.
So he joined us.
And the third cousin, who was left in Plaszow after we left, came to-- but accidentally came to Poland with-- he was a doctor.
He came escorting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a group of Poles coming, or Polish citizens coming back to Poland.
So he joined us also.
But he went back.
In this time, he was already [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a medical officer of Woomera.
So we went with him back-- the cousin, me and him.
To Germany, and we stayed there.
Yeah.
It was very interesting.
We stayed in a very small village, a small town in Bavaria.
This means house-- south-- south Germany.
And my cousin was in charge of a displaced persons hospital.
We stayed with him-- I, my cousin, and him.
And through a German doctor, we heard that the University of Erlangen, which is a very old one, will be the first one opened in-- in the next future.
And you should know it was August or July-- July or August '45.
So I, I, I wanted to study.
I wanted to use the time as profitable and as-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I wanted to-- to cope with the time, with the time I lost.
So I wanted to do something.
And the two possibilities were or to-- to go in business and on black market and to be one of the people who earned the money.
But then the second was, if it will be possible to learn, and after, I heard, or we heard-- the cousin as well-- that the university will open, we decided to try to get on it.
We didn't know how it-- how it will be.
We went there.
The transportation at that time was a very heavy one and very, very taboo.
It was a-- a distance of maybe 50 miles or 70 miles, something like this.
So it was a voyage of a full day, because nothing was working at that time.
We went by car, by train, by-- by-- by tracks, by whatever you could.
And we arrived in this Erlangen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there was a-- a management building of the university.
And I went in, and I said, what-- what about the opening?
So they said, we think we will be open-- opening in two or three months.
And today, we begin the inscription.
Who wants and who can, we'll inscribe, if we open.
And if the people will be accepted, so we will let know the people.
So what shall I do?
Fill-- I got such a packet of papers, and I was told to fill it.
Now, I saw every corner, it was a very big room, were standing all the people and filling out these papers.
I saw these papers, and the whole one page or two pages were personal datas.
All the others were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, only questions about what you did during the Nazi time, which party you belong to, and which youth organization you belong to.
And what political activities you do, because they wanted to find out who was a Nazi, because those wouldn't be accepted to-- to go to university.
So it was very, very, very detailed questions.
And I didn't know how to solve it.
So I filled out this personal data.
Took two minutes, three minutes.
Then I crossed very page and wrote-- through the cross-- I wrote, 'Jew.' [HEBREW].
That was my answer on this.
And after 5 or 10 minutes, I was through with all this, and I went to the-- the place where a lady clerk was sitting.
And she had to accept the papers back.
I was the first one who came.
And she looked at me like, it's impossible that you are already through with it, because it-- it takes a lot of time, you know.
These people had difficulties to fill out this-- all these very tricky questions.
So I, I, I, I, I, I felt that she looks at me, something must be wrong with this man, because he cannot be ready with this.
But I gave it to her.
And she looked at the first page, second page, third page ever.
And I saw that she became-- gets all the colors of-- which are possible on her face.
And she told me, wait a minute and disappeared into another room.
And then an older elderly man came out, very well-dressed, and he asked me to come into his office.
And he said he's the secretary of the university.
And I should know that I am the very first Jewish student after from certitude that I begin.
And I-- he's very moved with it.
I don't know if he really was.
Maybe he was moved in the negative way.
So he told me, I understand why you crossed this.
And I can only assure you in the moment that it will be-- that we will open, and you will be the first to know.
And so it was.
After two months, I think, or six weeks, they told me.
And in-- and at the end of November '45, I began to study.
